text: Just saw a LinkedIn Influencer with 'Organic Growth' written in the profile with 65K+ followers claiming that he can help you in growing your platform, copying the posts from other influencers.

Jobseekers, this one’s for you.
 Every application, every interview, every follow-up… the pressure is immense.
 And I know what you're thinking: Am I not good enough? 
 But let me tell you, this isn’t about you or your skills. It’s about a broken system where 60% of applicants never hear back. 
 Your mental health is not worth sacrificing for a system that doesn’t acknowledge your worth. 
 Please remember, taking care of yourself is the real priority. 
 Your dream job will come, but for now, breathe.
Looking for jobs on LinkedIn is like online dating: Full of promises, but in the end, you’re just left ghosted.

LinkedIn scams be like: 'Congratulations, you've been selected for a role you didn’t even apply for!' 
 The catch? Pay Rs. 50,000 for the honor.

sapne dekhna achi baat hai,
lekin job ka sapna dekh ke 'interested' likhna,
yeh toh achi baat nahi hai na?

Next time when I'll be reading some LinkedIn Influencer's story, I am starting from the last line.
If there's a link attached to it, it's most probably a fake one.
Saves me time!

Every time I poured my heart into 5-6 rounds of interviews and faced rejection, it felt like a punch in the gut.

But looking back, I realize it wasn’t nothing. It was the Universe’s way of saying, “Not this one, something better is on the way.”

Every single time, I’ve been shown why that rejection happened.

Doors I thought I wanted to walk through were shut, only to have the right ones swing open.

At first, it stung. But now, when things don’t go as planned, I don’t panic. I sit back, breathe, and trust.

To anyone feeling the weight of rejection: trust that the closed doors are protecting you from something you can’t see right now.

To everyone who's still looking for a job...

I see you. I feel you.

Every rejection email feels like a punch in the gut, and every 'We'll get back to you' sounds more like 'You'll never hear from us.'

But I want you to know, you're not alone in this.

According to a study, 80% of jobseekers struggle with anxiety and self-doubt during their search. It's normal to feel lost, but it's not the end.

Take breaks, breathe, and remember, this doesn't define you. Your worth is not tied to an offer letter.

Your mental health matters more than any job.

Sometimes, we forget that a company’s brand name doesn’t define someone’s talent.

I’ve had the privilege of working with people from smaller companies who blow me away with their skills and dedication.

I've seen the other side too—people in top-tier companies feeling lost, overwhelmed, or stuck, even though the world sees them as 'successful'.

Let’s stop attaching someone’s value to the company they work for.

Skills are what matter, not the size of the company behind them.

So when I left a toxic work environment, I told my manager a simple thing.

I just said: 'Hope your son gets a manager like you.'

She cannot be angry with me else it'll prove she was not a great manager.

Hope you all become the people that your sons/daughters will like to work under.

5 things nobody tells you about cracking a Product Manager interview:

1. Your resume is filtered by ATS before a human sees it
2. The STAR method is expected, not impressive
3. Case studies are won by structure, not brilliance
4. Culture fit matters as much as skills
5. Negotiation starts before the offer letter

Save this. Thank me later.

Hot take: Your GPA doesn't matter after your first job.

Recruiters don't ask. Managers don't care.

What actually gets you hired:
- The problems you've solved
- How you talk about your failures
- Whether you can think on your feet

Stop optimizing for grades. Start building things.

I got rejected from 47 companies before landing my dream job.

Here's what changed on attempt 48:

I stopped trying to sound impressive and started trying to be honest.

I talked about what went wrong in my last role. I admitted what I still don't know. I asked real questions about real problems.

Turns out, hiring managers are tired of polished robots too.

Be human. It's your biggest competitive advantage.

Unpopular opinion: Most productivity advice is made for people who already have their life together.

Wake up at 5am? Great, if you don't have a baby crying at 3am.
Meditate for 20 minutes? Sure, if your anxiety isn't paralyzing.
Journal daily? Lovely, if you can afford that kind of stillness.

Real productivity is figuring out what works for YOUR life, not copying someone else's morning routine.

Do what you can. It's enough.

The best career advice I ever got:

Don't optimize for salary. Optimize for learning.

The skills you build in your 20s will compound for decades. A high-paying job where you're learning nothing is a trap.

A lower-paying role where you grow fast will 10x your earning potential in 5 years.

Think in decades, not months.

3 signs your workplace is actually toxic (not just hard):

1. Your manager takes credit for your wins but blames you for failures
2. You feel dread every Sunday night — not just regular work stress
3. Asking for help is seen as weakness, not collaboration

Hard work is energizing. Toxicity is draining. Know the difference.

Your mental health is not a sacrifice you owe your employer.

I used to think I wasn't smart enough to be in tech.

I didn't go to a top college. I didn't have a strong network. I learned to code from YouTube.

Fast forward 3 years: I lead a team of 6 engineers at a funded startup.

The barrier to entry in tech isn't intelligence. It's consistency and the willingness to look stupid while you learn.

Anyone telling you otherwise is gatekeeping.

LinkedIn mein ek ajeeb cheez notice ki hai:

Jab koi success share karta hai — hazaaron likes.
Jab koi struggle share karta hai — sirf 'stay strong' comments.

Hum success celebrate karte hain, lekin struggle ko normalize nahi karte.

Aaj koi mushkil mein hai toh actually connect karo, bas ek emoji se kaam mat chalao.

Yaar, interview mein sabse bura moment kya hota hai?

Jab interviewer pooche: 'Where do you see yourself in 5 years?'

Aur tum soch rahe ho: 'Bhai, mujhe toh bas ye job chahiye pehle.'

Lekin bolte ho: 'I see myself in a leadership role, contributing to the company's vision.'

Hum sab actors hain LinkedIn pe.

Motivational speakers bolta hai: 'Fail fast, learn fast!'

Hum sochte hain: 'Theek hai bhai, try karte hain.'

Fir fail hote hain.

Fir koi motivational quote nahi aata dimag mein.

Bas yahi sochte hain: 'Ab kya hoga?'

Reality check: failure glamorous nahi hota. Lekin isse bahar aana possible hai — slowly, quietly, apne hisaab se.

AI is not going to take your job.

A person using AI effectively will.

The skill gap between people who embrace AI tools and those who ignore them is growing every quarter.

This isn't about replacing humans. It's about leverage.

One person with AI can now do what a team of 5 did two years ago.

Learn the tools. Adapt your workflow. The window to get ahead is open right now — but it won't stay open forever.

I reviewed 200 resumes last month. Here's what I wish candidates knew:

- 'Responsible for X' tells me nothing. 'Increased X by 30%' tells me everything.
- A 3-page resume for 2 years of experience is a red flag
- Spelling errors kill your chances instantly
- Generic summaries are skipped entirely
- Skills sections full of buzzwords with no proof are ignored

Quantify your impact. Be specific. Be ruthless with editing.

Your resume has 6 seconds. Make them count.

The loneliest part of entrepreneurship nobody posts about:

The days when you genuinely don't know if what you're building matters.

When the metrics are flat. When the team looks to you for energy you don't have. When you smile in meetings and stare at the ceiling at night.

This is the job. And it doesn't get easier — you just get better at sitting with the uncertainty.

To every founder going through this right now: you're not failing. You're just in the middle of the story.

Startup culture glorifies hustle.

But here's what 80-hour weeks actually produce:
- Burned out engineers who write buggy code
- Managers too exhausted to give real feedback
- Decisions made on ego, not data

The best companies I've seen move fast AND protect their people's time.

Speed is a strategy. Burnout is a bug.

Fresh out of college and feeling behind? Read this.

Your peers who got placed in 'big companies' are not ahead of you.

They're on a different path — not a better one.

The person who spends 2 years building real skills at a startup will outpace the person coasting in a big company within 5 years.

Stop comparing timelines. Start comparing growth rates.

Leadership lesson I learned the hard way:

You can't inspire people you don't actually know.

I used to lead with strategy decks and quarterly goals. My team respected me but didn't trust me.

Then I started asking one question every 1:1: 'What's making your work harder than it needs to be?'

Everything changed.

The best leaders don't have all the answers. They ask better questions.

10 saal ki corporate life ke baad jo seekha:

1. Jo loudly bol sakta hai, woh promote hota hai
2. Hard work aur visibility — dono chahiye
3. Apna kaam khud promote karo, koi nahi karega
4. Manager se relationship zyada important hai skills se (sad but true)
5. Kabhi bhi sirf ek company pe depend mat karo

Yeh sab school mein nahi sikhate. Isliye share kar raha hoon.

Kya aapko pata hai LinkedIn pe kitne log fake success stories share karte hain?

'6 months mein 10 lakh kamaye' — without mentioning the 5 lakh invested.
'Zero se hero' — without mentioning family support.

Real success bohot quieter hoti hai.

Jo genuinely aage badh rahe hain, woh usually LinkedIn pe brag nahi karte.

Dhoka mat khao yahan ke narratives se. Apni journey likho.

Nobody talks about the mental load of a job search.

It's not just sending CVs.

It's researching every company. Tailoring every cover letter. Preparing for every interview format. Managing rejection after rejection. Explaining the gap to your parents. Pretending you're fine.

If you're in this right now — it's okay to not be okay.

This phase is hard. And it will end.

The most underrated career skill in 2024: Learning in public.

Share what you're building. Document what you're learning. Post your failures alongside your wins.

Why it works:
- It forces clarity in your thinking
- It attracts opportunities you weren't looking for
- It builds trust faster than any portfolio

You don't need to be an expert to share. You just need to be one step ahead of someone.

Ek baat poochu? Genuinely.

Kitne log hain jo subah uthke khush hote hain apni job ke liye?

Agar answer hai 'bahut kam' — toh yeh sochne wali baat hai.

Hum 8-10 ghante din ka sabse important kaam mein lagate hain. Agar woh dil se nahi ho raha, toh energy kahan se aayegi?

Apne aap se poochho: main yahan kyu hoon? Aur kya main wahan hona chahta hoon?
